front: Motet: 40-voice work scored for eight choirs of five voices each; text from the Book of Judith ending 'respice humilitatem nostram'
Motet: possibly written for Elizabeth I's 40th birthday; may have been commissioned to outdo Alessandro Striggio's Ecce beatam lucem
Motet: eight choirs arranged in a horseshoe around the audience; melodies pass between choirs before all forty voices unite
Motet: Janet Cardiff's contemporary installation plays the forty parts from separate speakers; featured permanently at the National Gallery of Canada
Choral Work: 1910 work by Ralph Vaughan Williams for double string orchestra and string quartet, based on Tallis's Phrygian psalm tune; premiered at the Three Choirs Festival
back: Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis (Ralph Vaughan Williams)
work: Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis (Vaughan Williams)

front: Choral Work: divides the string orchestra into three sections of differing size; opens with two orchestras playing pianissimo e molto sostenuto
back: Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis (Ralph Vaughan Williams)
work: Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis (Vaughan Williams)

front: Choral Work: Tallis's Phrygian setting of Psalm 2; the third of nine tunes written for Archbishop Matthew Parker's Psalter
back: "Why Fum'th in Fight" / Third Mode Melody (Thomas Tallis)
work: "Why Fum'th in Fight" / Third Mode Melody / Tallis Canon

front: Choral Work: hymn tune 'Glory to thee, my God, this night' is taken from this Tallis work also in Archbishop Parker's Psalter
work: "Why Fum'th in Fight" / Third Mode Melody / Tallis Canon

front: Motet: two settings of Tenebrae lessons for Maundy Thursday; scored ATTBB; each verse begins with a Hebrew letter
Motet: Richard Terry argued in Downside Review that this work showed Tallis had lifelong allegiance to the Roman Catholic church
Motet: joint collection of 34 motets by Tallis and William Byrd, published 1575; dedicated to Elizabeth I; was a commercial failure
Composer: Elizabeth I granted this composer and his student William Byrd a joint 21-year monopoly on printing polyphonic music in 1575
Composer: Byrd wrote the elegy 'Ye Sacred Muses' to commemorate this composer's death
Composer: a cappella ensemble named after this composer, founded by Peter Phillips; records on Gimell Records; helped repopularize early vocal music
Motet: early antiphon to the Virgin Mary in the Phrygian mode; motifs reused in a five-part mass of the same name
Composer: served the Chapel Royal through four Tudor monarchs, adapting religious style from Catholic polyphony to Protestant psalm tunes
Composer: 'If ye love me' — four-part syllabic anthem setting a vernacular text from the Gospel of John
Composer: O nata lux de lumine — notable for a cross relationship (false relation) in the final cadence, with the top voice singing F-sharp to G against a descending F–E-flat–D in the tenor
Composer: Short Service in the Dorian Mode — includes Magnificat and Nunc Dimittis sections
Motet: Egerton and Gresham manuscripts are its principal sources; structure may have been inspired by the architecture of Nonsuch Palace
Motet: its length may reflect the gematria value of 'Judith' and the composer's surname, both equal to 69; Peter Gregson used multitracking to perform all 40 voices on a single cello
Motet: recorded by the Kronos Quartet for their album Black Angels
Choral Work: premiered at the 1910 Three Choirs Festival held at Gloucester Cathedral
back: Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis (Ralph Vaughan Williams)
work: Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis (Vaughan Williams)

front: Composer: Christmas Mass 'Puer natus est nobis' — written for Mary I, possibly in preparation for the hoped birth of an heir
Composer: also wrote a Miserere nostri
